infoPages.title: My Second Brain
infoPages.content:
🌵OldMartijntje's Basement

On January 16th, 2024, I started using Obsidian during an internship. It was supposed to be just for work notes, but it quickly became something way more important: my second brain.

Everything goes in there. School notes, programming tips, random stories I come up with, a todo list (that I definitely forget to look at), you name it. If I thought about it, there's probably a note about it.

Why host it publicly?

Shortly after starting, I discovered Quartz (v4.1.5 back then, now on v4.5.0) and thought "why not share this mess with the world?" So I did. Now it lives at docs.oldmartijntje.nl as 🌵OldMartijntje's Basement.

The coolest part? People actually read it. I once wrote an article about a school project, and exactly one year later, I saw 5 people reading it - probably the next batch of students working on the same project. That's when I realized this wasn't just for me anymore.

Without Obsidian, I'd have forgotten half the things I learned. Especially in classes like data science where you hear 20 new terms per minute. Now I just write it down, and future me can deal with it.

infoPages.title: System Changes
infoPages.content:
My Vault's Journey

Your brain keeps changing constantly, so why shouldn't my second brain? Over the past two years, my organizational system has completely transformed multiple times.

The Dump Era

At first, it was chaos. Just a folder full of notes with no real structure. It worked... until it didn't.

Johnny Decimal System

Then I tried the Johnny Decimal system. Everything got numbered: 23.00 Easy Webhosting guide, 23.01 HTML CheatSheet, and so on. Look at this organization:

But here's the problem: I started writing for other people instead of myself. My vault became a wannabe Wikipedia instead of a personal knowledge base. Plus, I just dumped everything into an "unsorted" folder anyway, defeating the entire purpose. You can read about why I ditched it here.

Format-Based System

Next attempt: organizing by note format. Folders like "list of things," "article," and "short-note." It was better, but still not quite right.

Zettelkasten (Current)

On November 5th, 2025, a friend showed me a video about Zettelkasten, and everything clicked. Every note gets a unique ID that never changes, even if I rename or move the note. No more broken links.

Read about my Zettelkasten setup →

infoPages.title: Growth & Features
infoPages.content:
Watching It Grow

The best way to see how much I've written? Look at my knowledge graph over time:

Mid 2024
Start 2025
Mid 2025
December 2025

You can see I wrote a TON in the second half of 2025. And those graphs don't even show links to non-existing articles. Here's what it looks like in the Obsidian app:

Obsidian Link Syntax

In Obsidian, you can link to articles that don't exist yet using [[Article Name]]. If the article exists, the link works. If it doesn't, it shows up grayed out - reminding me to write it eventually. This is why you might hit a 404 on my site; it's just a note I haven't written yet.

QuartzForums

In December 2025, I added discussion forums to every article using QuartzForums. Now people can comment on my notes, ask questions, or just chat. Check it out →

Recent Decorations

I've also started making things prettier. Like adding TEMMIE from Undertale to my homepage because why not?

My old homepage vs...

...my current homepage with TEMMIE

infoPages.title: Zettel Search & 404s
infoPages.content:
Dealing with 404s

Here's the thing: if you bookmark one of my articles, it might lead to a 404 someday. That happens when I rename, move, or delete notes. But there are solutions!

Zettel Search

Every Zettelkasten note has a permanent ID that never changes. If you really want to keep a note, copy both the link and the Zettelkasten ID. If it 404s, just search for the ID and you'll find it at its new location.

Other Options

  • Use the Internet Archive
  • Ask in QuartzForums where a note moved to
  • Use the search function on my site

The Homepage

The homepage always shows Recent notes from the last month - both newly created pages marked as NEW and updates to existing articles marked as EDITED. This is why "Index" has the most links in my graph - everything points back to it.

infoPages.title: Real Life Impact
infoPages.content:
How It Changed My Life

Obsidian makes my life so much easier. Before, when I got a new PC, I'd forget half the apps I had installed. Now? I just check my app list.

This note shows exactly which apps I use on Ubuntu and where I downloaded them from. I also have notes about:

  • Ubuntu settings I've changed
  • Windows app lists (from when I used Windows)
  • Custom configurations and tweaks

If I ever switch back to Windows or set up a new Ubuntu machine, I can get everything running in no time.

Always Taking Notes

These days, I bring my laptop everywhere just to take notes. Even if it's not useful right now, it might be useful later - or to someone else. That's the beauty of a public second brain.

I Love Markdown

Writing in markdown is just... *chef's kiss*. Clean, simple, portable. Everything is just text files that'll work forever.

Explore my vault →

infoPages.content:
This is my old website which is just a dump where I used to test stuff, or just place random things that I thought would be fun.
This website has different homepages dependign on your device, but it is intended for Desktop users.
It had a whole Achievement / easteregg finding gig.

The design analysis behind this website is not: "Is this a good place for this?", No it goes like: "Hmm where should I place this big clicker game, hmm 1 small button between anything else is a good idea."
This way it has lots of secrets to find.

Easteregg collector.

This website is made with Angular, PHP (backend that might be offline now), MySQL, and a lot of love.

It can run Doom.

This was my main website for a long time, but I have moved on to a new website, but I still keep this one up for the memories.
The reason I switched websites is because it is not really easy access to information like "My projects" etc, and I made the new website at the same time I got my VPS, which made it easy to combine front and back-end.

infoPages.title: Main
infoPages.content:
This is a website I made as a summer project.

✨Totally Normal Chess Simulator✨

This project started as a normal chess game (without En passant / castling), Reddit had the power to decide what would be added in the next update. Normal chess things or not, didn't matter.
This happened 7 times:

  1. Added Microtransactions
  2. Added Lootboxes
  3. Added Merging Pieces
  4. Added carrying pieces
  5. Added a twist selector
  6. Added a tech tree
  7. Added more pawn varients

Anyone can play this game for free. It's a fun project that I'm proud of.

Tech tree.

Technologies used:

  • HTML
  • CSS
  • JavaScript

You can play this on mobile or desktop, it even has settings to suit your preferences.

infoPages.title: Main Hub
infoPages.content:
OldMartijntje.nl

Odds are you're currently on this website right now. This is my main hub - where I showcase projects, manage my database, write blog posts, and generally do all the cool stuff.

Learning Experience

This was my first time building a project with a bunch of technologies:

  • Node Backend - finally ditched static files
  • Account system - with all the security headaches that come with it
  • CRUD Editor - so I can manage everything without touching code
  • React - component heaven
  • Bootstrap - making things look decent quickly

Marvel Inspiration

I was heavily inspired by Marvel's Agents of S.H.I.E.L.D. when building this. You'll see clearance levels, agent-style interfaces, and a whole vibe that screams "secret organization."

Accounts have clearance levels, just like in S.H.I.E.L.D.

infoPages.title: Features & Secrets
infoPages.content:
Account System

Accounts on this website are invite only. But don't worry - there's a guest account button if you just want to look around without committing.

More Marvel-inspired vibes on the signup page

Oh, and if you manage to get an invite link, the register page lets you use some... interesting characters in your username.

Yes, you can use 🌵✨🗿 in your username. Because why not?

M.A.R.A. OS Easter Egg

Hidden in the website is M.A.R.A. OS - a fake CRT computer system. You can't do much in it except find like 3 files and run a few commands (ls, info), but it even has authentic screen burn-in effects like old CRT monitors.

Running commands in M.A.R.A. OS

I was planning to make this a bigger thing, but it remains a fun little easter egg for now.

infoPages.title: Project Showcase
infoPages.content:
What's On Display

The main purpose of this website is to show off all my projects. From website tools to games to random experiments.

You'll find things like:

  • QuartzForums - the forum system for my Obsidian vault
  • SkinForge - Minecraft skin layerer
  • Chess Simulator 256 - exactly what it sounds like
  • Project Kazamata - game project
  • And a bunch more weird stuff

Blog Posts

I also write small blog posts here about whatever's going on. Updates, life stuff, random thoughts. It's not as extensive as my Obsidian vault, but it's nice to have a casual spot to write.

Events System (RIP)

There was an events system I was building, but I stopped working on it and recently disabled the button. Maybe someday I'll revive it. Maybe not. We'll see :3

infoPages.title: Behind The Scenes
infoPages.content:
Managing Content

This website also serves as my content management system. I can edit the displayed projects and blog posts directly from here, which makes updating everything super convenient.

The editing interface - functional over fancy

Fun Data Endpoints

I also manage data for a couple of fun endpoints:

  • Picture Album - stores all the pictures I've taken with my little camera
  • Random News Messages - inspired by Antimatter Dimensions, these are random funny messages

You can actually see the random news messages in action in my unfinished Roblox game. They just pop up and add some personality to whatever project I throw them into.

There are big plans for this website. But we'll see if they happen or not :3

Visit the website →

infoPages.title: Main
infoPages.content:
Project Betacraft Explorer is a unique Minecraft playthrough where I start from Alpha v1.0.1_01 and progress through the game's versions. Here's how it works:

  1. I use Betacraft to play older versions of Minecraft.
  2. Every time I reopen Minecraft, I update the game by one minor version (excluding April Fools versions).
  3. If I die, I restart the game in a newer version.
  4. I log my progress for each version in a diary.
  5. Each version is played for a maximum of 30 minutes (until we reach the official releases).
  6. If I'm watching YouTube while playing, I switch versions when I need to find a new video (or based on the above rules if the video is longer).

To keep track of my progress and ensure I don't lose any data, I've set up an automatic backup system:

An interesting bug discovery during the playthrough.

I created a Python script to automatically commit changes to a Git repository. The script uses Git LFS (Large File Storage) to handle the potentially large world files efficiently. You can find the script in my Python toolkit repository.

Screenshot of a time where my Minecraft crashed.

Throughout this project, I've encountered various interesting situations, from game crashes to unusual glitches. For example, in version 13w02a-1613, my game crashed because my pickaxe broke while I was digging a tunnel.

The exciting moment of finding my first diamonds!

As of the latest entry (1.5), I've made significant progress, including finding my first diamonds and completing a major excavation project. It is fun to play through this nostalgic trip and see how much Minecraft has changed over time. I still have a long way to go, Cause Minecraft is at 1.21.2 at the time of this post.

infoPages.title: Main
infoPages.content:
The Roblox Maze Generator is my first project using LUA and Roblox. It's an automatic maze generation script with a wide array of settings, ensuring that every maze is unique and fully accessible.

  1. Automatic Generation: The script creates mazes without manual input.
  2. Full Accessibility: Every location in the maze is always reachable.
  3. Biomes: The maze features different themed areas or "biomes" with unique generation rules.
  4. Randomization: Each generated maze is completely unique.
  5. Customizable Settings: Various parameters can be adjusted to change the maze characteristics.

The maze features different biomes, each with its own generation rules:

  • Hallways: Strict, narrow passages.
  • Rooms: More open areas with randomly placed walls.
A glimpse inside the "rooms" biome, showcasing the open areas with random wall placement.

The development process was filled with interesting challenges and discoveries:

Very early in development, showing the basic structure.

Initially, the project started with simple hallways, but after a while biomes and other variations have been added.

Testing the implementation of vertical hallways, adding a new dimension to the maze.

One of the key features of the generator is its ability to create multi-level mazes, adding vertical complexity to the traditional 2D maze concept.

A funny lighting bug encountered during development, showcasing the unexpected challenges in game development.

Like any development project, there were interesting bugs along the way. Some, like this lighting issue, created unexpectedly beautiful results!

Future plans for the Roblox Maze Generator include:

  • Adding "monsters" to increase challenge and excitement.
  • Implementing a goal-oriented gameplay, giving players a reason to find the exit.

Currently, the maze has only one exit, but the possibilities for expansion are endless. The Roblox Maze Generator represents not just a technical achievement in procedural generation, but also a journey of learning and problem-solving in game development using LUA and Roblox.

infoPages.title: Introduction
infoPages.content:
📦OldMartijntje's Toolkit

Welcome to 📦OldMartijntje's Toolkit, a collection of various tools that range from handy utilities to just-for-fun experiments. It's an online Node.js package that you can host yourself for access to even more advanced tools and features. Think of it as your own digital Swiss Army knife, packed with everything from chatrooms to file patchers.

Want to explore further? Feel free to browse through each tool below. If you're looking for a quick start, dive right into our Easy Navmap and find the tool that suits your needs.

Track the weeks you’ve lived and compare them to different activities.

This toolkit has something for everyone. Hosted online, it offers a quick and accessible experience, but you can also host it locally to unlock additional features.

Let’s dive into the tools available in the toolkit!

infoPages.title: Fun & Reflective Tools
infoPages.content:
📦OldMartijntje's Toolkit - Fun & Reflective Tools

This page explores some of the more engaging tools in the Toolkit. Whether you’re calculating your lived weeks or joining a chatroom, there’s something to pique your curiosity.

Weeks Lived Tracker: Reflect on your past weeks, and even add notes for each week to revisit memories. It’s also fun to see how many times you could’ve cycled from London to Amsterdam!
Chatroom with MQTT: Chat in real-time with others, join or create rooms, and check out past contacts.

These tools are designed for reflection and connection, giving you ways to look back, share thoughts, or chat with others in real-time.

infoPages.title: Utility Tools
infoPages.content:
📦OldMartijntje's Toolkit - Utility Tools

On this page, we’ll highlight some of the practical tools you might find useful for various tasks, from file comparisons to markdown conversions.

MQTT Receiver: Monitor MQTT traffic, mimic or automate messages, and analyze message frequency when hosted offline.
Character Replacement Tool: Swap characters in text to create puzzles or experiment with variations.
Markdown to Word Converter: Quickly convert markdown text into formatted Word documents.

Additional tools include:

  • File Patcher: Insert two files and get a patch showing the differences. Useful for code versioning.
  • Character Counter: Detect invisible or unusual characters for a quick quality check.

These tools make tasks easier, faster, and more accessible, especially when hosted locally for enhanced capabilities.

infoPages.title: Intro
infoPages.content:
Welcome to SkinForge!

SkinForge is a Minecraft skin layerer I made to make remixing, customizing, and creating skins fun and effortless. No more tedious pixel editing—just pick, preview, and export!

You can add layers, reorder them, toggle visibility, and instantly see your changes in both 2D and 3D. The built-in library and easy import options make it simple to get started.

Want to know how it works? Check out the next tab!

I made this project for everyone who loves Minecraft.




It is not made for phones (yet?)

infoPages.title: How it works
infoPages.content:
How Does SkinForge Work?

Start with a base skin and add layers from the library or your own files.

Simply pick a base (like Steve), then add faces, hair, accessories, and more. Drag layers to reorder, toggle them on/off, or adjust opacity. You can even upload your own PNG layers with the gray button below the layers.

Toggle layers to instantly see the difference—here, Steve's beard is hidden.
Adjust opacity for creative effects—Steve's hair is now half transparent!

When you're happy, export your skin as a Minecraft PNG, or save your progress for later. It's all instant, in your browser.

infoPages.title: Library
infoPages.content:
Explore the SkinForge Library

Browse hundreds of layers—hair, faces, accessories, suits, and more.
Switch categories or use the search bar to find exactly what you want. The library is always fast and easy to use!

Just click to add any layer to your skin. All items are credited to their creators, so you can discover new artists and styles.

Try it out here.

infoPages.title: Customization Examples
infoPages.content:
Pick & Choose: Endless Customization

Mix and match: new hair, new face, cat ears, gloves, headphones—just click to add!
Layers blend together—add a suit, adjust opacity, and your skin is instantly unique.

With SkinForge, you can create anything: from classic Steve to wild new looks. Every change is instant, and you can always undo or tweak your layers.

infoPages.title: Import & Export
infoPages.content:
Export, Save, and Credit Creators

When you export, SkinForge shows credits for every creator whose work you used.
Save your progress as a .skinforge file, or export a PNG for Minecraft. Embedded saves ensure your skin stays the same, even if the library updates.

You can always come back and edit your skin later. Old save files are supported, and you can choose to embed custom layers for perfect preservation.

See it in action here.

infoPages.title: Intro
infoPages.content:
⚠️ Discontinued: QuartzForums is no longer displayed on the docs.oldmartijntje website.

QuartzForums

So I was using Obsidian to organize my brain, then I hosted it with Quartz, and one day I thought: "wouldn't it be cool if people could discuss stuff on each page?"

That's how QuartzForums was born. It's a forum system that lives beneath your articles. No moderation from me, no control—just pure community discussion.

The idea

I built a plugin for Quartz that adds a discussion section to every article in my vault. Then I made it universal so literally anyone could use it on their own Quartz site (or eventually, anywhere else).

Head over to 🌵OldMartijntje's Basement and scroll to the bottom of any article. You'll see a little disclaimer, hit proceed, and boom—forums unlocked.

infoPages.title: How to Use
infoPages.content:
Getting Started

Step 1: Create an Account

First, head to api.oldmartijntje.nl/quartzforums and create an account. You'll get an access key—this is your ticket to posting.

Step 2: Add Your Key

Go back to any article on docs.oldmartijntje.nl, click the auth settings button in the forum section, and paste your access key.

Step 3: Start Discussing

Now you can post messages! You've got:

  • Full markdown support
  • Ability to reply to other messages (blockquotes included)
  • Helpful buttons for formatting
  • Links automatically turn into wikilinks (local only)

Your Profile

On your profile page, you can reset your access key if you leaked it, add a footer to your messages, see all your discussions, and even delete your account if you want out.

infoPages.title: Explore Forums
infoPages.content:
Browsing All Forums

Want to see what people are discussing? Head to api.oldmartijntje.nl/quartzforums

You can:

  • Browse all recent discussions
  • Filter by website (future-proofing for when this gets embedded elsewhere)
  • Check out other users' profiles and their activity
  • Delete your own messages (they'll show as [Deleted User])

A Quick Note

If an article gets renamed or moved in Obsidian, its forum is archived but still viewable at the old location (which might be a 404 now). It's a quirk of the system, but it keeps discussions safe.

Read my full write-up on QuartzForums →

infoPages.title: Intro
infoPages.content:
Avans Elective Hub

This was a school project for Avans Year 2 Period 1. The goal? Build an app that helps students find elective modules that actually match their interests.

Built with NestJS (backend) and React (frontend), using MongoDB for storage. It's not publicly hosted anymore since I got my grade, but you can totally self-host it if you want.

Getting it running

Clone the repos:

  • Frontend
  • Backend

Run npm run setup in the backend to walk through the setup steps. Then start the backend with npm run start and the frontend with npm run dev.

The Cool Part

Everything is fully translatable. Every UI element, every module description—all of it can be translated through the interface. No hardcoded strings anywhere.

infoPages.title: Student Features
infoPages.content:
What Students Can Do

Your Dashboard

When you log in, you land on your personal dashboard. Here you can select your main study, check out recommended modules, browse your favorites, and see your info.

Finding Modules

The system recommends modules based on your study's tags. It's a simple tag-matching algorithm for now (the plan was to use AI in the final product, but this was just a mockup).

You can also browse all modules with filters for study credits, tags, and module level. Favorite anything you like, and view detailed info on any module.

infoPages.title: Admin Panel
infoPages.content:
Admin & Teacher Tools

Teachers and admins get access to a management panel with three main tools:

Translation Management

Edit all UI translations and clean up orphaned translations. Orphaned translations happen when you change a module's text—the old translation sticks around until you remove it. All UI translations are cached for speed.

Subject Management

Full CRUD for modules (subjects). You can filter by anything, and edit all the details of a module.

Fun detail: multiple subjects can share the same translation to save database space. Not that MongoDB really needs it, but I was experimenting.

Study Management

Manage all study programs—add new ones, edit existing ones, or remove outdated programs.

infoPages.title: What is it?
infoPages.content:
BibleDownloader

I wanted to use Bible text for a project (spoiler: never got around to it), so I made a tool to download entire Bibles. I found this MATLAB repo that did something similar, forked it, and completely remade it in Node.js because... why not?

What can it download?

BibleDownloader can grab 6 public domain Bibles from bible.com:

  • ASV - American Standard Version (English)
  • KJV - King James Version (English)
  • WEB - World English Bible (English)
  • SV1750 - Statenvertaling 1750 (Nederlands)
  • LS1910 - Louis Segond 1910 (Français)
  • RV1602 - Reina Valera 1602 (Español)

Why self-hosted?

The tool also technically supports some copyrighted Bibles (like NASB, Basisbijbel, Het Boek), but you're only allowed to download those if you physically own them. It's a gray area, so I made it self-hosted to keep things safe and let users take responsibility for their own usage.

infoPages.title: How to use
infoPages.content:
Getting Started

Installation

Clone the repo from GitHub, install dependencies, and run:

npm run start

Choose Your Bible

Select a Bible translation, pick your format (JSON or HTML), and choose a download speed:

  • Fast - Quick downloads, multiple concurrent calls
  • Balanced - Middle ground between speed and safety
  • Conservative - Slower, with built-in sleep to avoid rate limits

Download Process

The app downloads everything to your local application first. You can watch the progress in real-time, and once it's done, you'll get a .zip file ready to download.

The UI is available in English and Dutch, so pick whichever you're comfortable with.

infoPages.title: Legal stuff
infoPages.content:
Important Legal Notice

This is the legal notice from the original repo I forked. I'm not a legal expert, so if you think my legal logic is wrong, please let me know and I'll take action accordingly.

Under Dutch law, downloading a copy of a copyright-protected work is allowed if you are a natural person (i.e., a human, not acting for a corporation) and the copy is for personal (home) use only, under the condition that you already own an otherwise legally acquired copy.

WARNING: The law of your jurisdiction might be different. It may prohibit the use of this script or set different requirements.

Users must take full legal responsibility for the use of this application and the files it generates. By using this application, you agree to these terms and confirm that your use is legal in your jurisdiction.

TL;DR

The 6 public domain Bibles? Go wild. The copyrighted ones? Only if you physically own them. Piracy is not allowed.

Check out the project on GitHub →

infoPages.title: Intro
infoPages.content:
diagonal-dinosaur

So you want to spin up a NodeTS + EJS + MongoDB webapp but don't want to spend hours setting up boilerplate? Yeah, me too. That's why I made diagonal-dinosaur.

It's a template that gets you from zero to a working web app in minutes. Dashboard, authentication, database—all ready to go. Just run the setup script and start building your actual features.

What's in the box?

This template was born from my old board-game-statistics project. I stripped out the game stats stuff and kept the good parts: the authentication system, the setup flow, and a clean dashboard.

It works with MongoDB (local, self-hosted, or Atlas) and comes with a setup script that handles all the boring .env and settings.json stuff for you.

infoPages.title: Setup & Features
infoPages.content:
Getting Started

Run npm i then npm run setup. The setup script walks you through everything:

  • Database connection: Point it to your MongoDB instance
  • Port selection: Choose where to host (4200, 3000, 4242, or custom)
  • Account creation mode: Old POST method or modern UI-based registration
  • Admin account: Create your first user right away

Account Creation Options

You can allow unlimited registrations (anyone can sign up) or single user only (just you). The first account gets the special # accessIdentifier—basically superuser access.

Request Logging

By default, every HTTP request gets logged to daily files under root/logging/{year}/{month}/{day}.log. You can turn this off in advanced setup if you want.

infoPages.title: Access & Customization
infoPages.content:
AccessIdentifiers

Instead of simple clearance levels, this template uses accessIdentifiers. Think of them as tags that control what users can see.

Say you want friends to access one section and family to access another—clearance levels don't work for that. But with accessIdentifiers like friend or family, you can gate pages however you want.

Customizing Your Dashboard

Adding or removing dashboard tabs is straightforward:

  • Create an EJS view file (e.g., dashboardSettings.ejs)
  • Add a route in server.ts
  • Update the sidebar navigation in dashboard.ejs

The template is designed to be ripped apart and rebuilt. It's just a starting point.

Fun Facts

There's also a JavaScript version called vertical-dinosaur, but it's discontinued. And honestly, diagonal-dinosaur probably won't get many updates either—it's a template, not a product.

But hey, it's super useful. This is where I started making setup scripts for all my Node.js backends. No more forgetting what the .env structure looks like or manually typing out connection strings.

Check it out on GitHub →

I keep forgetting to update this website with my newest projects. Slowly but surely I'll add my backlog of 6 projects.
This is a backlog of about 12 months of creation? And this excludes projects that I stopped with halfway through.


Progress: 6/6

infoPages.title: The Problem
infoPages.content:
A-90 Detection Tool

I suck at games that require fast reaction times. But I really wanted to beat DOORS, this Roblox horror game where you navigate through 100 rooms to reach the end.

In DOORS, there's a subarea called "The Rooms" with 1000 rooms. And in those rooms, there's this absolute menace called A-90.

What is A-90?

A-90 is a pixelated, distorted face that randomly appears on your screen starting around room 90. When it shows up, you hear a knock, see its face somewhere on your screen, and then BAM—red static and a stop sign.

Here's the catch: if you move at all—camera, character, even hiding—it jumpscares you and deals 90 damage. You have to completely freeze for a few seconds.

For someone with slow reactions like me? Yeah, that's a problem.

infoPages.title: How to use
infoPages.content:
How It Works

So I wrote a Python script that constantly monitors your screen for A-90. When it detects the entity, it deselects your Roblox window—meaning you literally can't move. You're safe.

The tool opens a popup when it finds A-90, and since DOORS doesn't require much clicking, you're pretty much golden. Just click back into the game after the entity passes.

Why Multiple Images?

A-90's appearance can be distorted by game filters or overlays. That's why the tool comes with 7 default images—some are zoomed-in versions of others to catch different scenarios. You can test the tool beforehand with the included test images to make sure detection works properly on your setup.

And hey, this isn't just for A-90. Throw any image into the images/ folder and it'll detect that too.

AI helped in the creation of this application :3

infoPages.title: Tips & Customization
infoPages.content:
Getting Started

Requirements

You'll need Python 3.12 (older versions might work, but no promises) and these packages:

  • cv2
  • mss

Keybinds

When the monitor window is selected:

  • Q - Quit the app
  • D - Debug mode
  • S - Save the current frame

The Popup

When A-90 is detected, a popup appears and automatically focuses. There's a button to disable scanning for 1 minute (useful if you get softlocked). You can also use Tab and Enter to navigate the buttons.

Important Notes

  • Only detects on your main screen
  • Test with the provided test images before playing
  • Add multiple image variations for better detection
  • Game filters/overlays might affect detection accuracy

Check out the project on GitHub →

infoPages.title: Info & Tools
infoPages.content:
GameingGrounds_Sandbox

A Fabric mod for Minecraft 1.21.1 I worked on with a friend. Here's the stuff I added—because this is my website and I'm showing off what I made.

Check out the GitHub repo if you want to see more or download it.

The Hammer - 3x3 Mining

Ever wished you could mine faster? I added a hammer that breaks blocks in a 3x3 area. It's technically a pickaxe, so it only works on stone-type blocks.

Bedrock Drill - Actually Break Bedrock

The vanilla bedrock breaking method is tedious and annoying. So I added a drill. It won't make it cheaper though—it still costs the same as the traditional method.

How it works:

  • Click on bedrock: 2 HP damage to the drill
  • Click on TNT: Removes the TNT and restores 1 HP
  • Crafting gives you only 2/32 HP to start
  • You can use it with 1 HP left (it'll work for that final click)

Red Sugar Ore

Inspired by Deep Rock Galactic, this ore has a 10% chance to give you regeneration when mined. It spawns in both normal stone and deepslate.

infoPages.title: Trading Cards
infoPages.content:
Trading Cards System

I added a full trading card system to Minecraft. Because why not? You can find card packs, open them, and use the cards for various abilities.

Card Packs

Each pack has different luck levels (like Lucky III) that affect what you'll get inside.

The Cards

Cards have rarities (Common to Legendary), conditions (Mint, Near-Mint, etc.), and abilities with cooldowns. When you use a card, its condition degrades slightly.

After using a card, it goes on cooldown. Stack multiple cards in one slot and their effects stack too!

Cards track who discovered them and can have card sleeves applied (though that's not shown in these screenshots).

infoPages.title: Armor Trims
infoPages.content:
Custom Armor Trims

I added two custom armor trims and expanded the materials you can use with all trims (vanilla included).

Questionable Trim

If you know, you know. It's an Among Us reference. Currently uncraftable, so you can't duplicate the template.

Fury Trim

Inspired by old-school Minecraft skins with that classic edgy look. You know the ones.

New Trim Materials

Added six new materials you can use to color armor trims: Nether Star, Slime Ball, Fire Charge, Ender Pearl, Honeycomb, and Echo Shard.

These work with both custom and vanilla trims, giving you way more customization options.

infoPages.title: Extras
infoPages.content:
Custom Paintings & Music

Legacy Music Disc

Added a music disc for "Kitsune Maison" by Porter Robinson. It's a "legacy" disc—meaning it exists in the game but doesn't actually play sound due to copyright reasons. It's more of a collectible at this point.

Custom Paintings

I made three custom paintings using the Exposure mod:

Forgplort (4x4) - A frogport from the Create mod. Yes, that's what I named it.

Afar (2x2) - A distant waystone view.

Psychedelic (4x4) - Made using chromatic aberration for that trippy effect.

Check out the full mod on GitHub →

infoPages.title: What is it?
infoPages.content:
Commit-grabber

Ever wondered what your most common commit message is? Or wanted to make your own GitHub Wrapped style thing?

That's exactly why I made Commit-grabber. It's a tiny Python script that grabs all your GitHub commits and dumps them into a CSV file. Perfect for doing data science on your own coding habits.

What does it do?

It connects to the GitHub API, pulls all your commits from all your repos, and organizes them into a neat commit_details.csv file. Then you can load it into a Jupyter notebook and analyze away.

Want to see how many commits you make at 3am? Or which repos you've been neglecting? Or if your commit messages are getting lazier over time? Now you can.

infoPages.title: How to use
infoPages.content:
Setup & Usage

Step 1: Get your API key

Head to github.com/settings/tokens and create a classic token with repo access.

Step 2: Create a .env file

In your project folder, create a .env file with: 

API_KEY=ghp_00000000000000000
GH_NAME=username

Step 3: Run it

Just run run.py and it'll spit out your commit_details.csv. Load it into a Jupyter notebook and start exploring your data.

For example, here's the distribution of my commit message lengths. Most of my commits are between 0-10 words long (because who has time to write essays?).

Check it out on GitHub →

infoPages.title: What is it?
infoPages.content:
CodeDungeon - Learn Python by Playing

CodeDungeon is a Python game where you control your character by writing actual code. Inspired by robotarm projects my school used and the classic PixelDungeon, I wanted to make learning to code feel like playing a game.

How it works

Every dungeon is procedurally generated based on a seed. You write Python commands to move, attack, and interact. The game runs on Python 3.10+ and needs PIL (Pillow) installed. It uses tkinter for the window, not pygame.

The game has my custom account system built in, which lets you share achievements and cosmetics across different Python games I've made.

infoPages.title: Playing & Enemies
infoPages.content:
Writing Commands

The template is simple—import the system, start the game, write your commands, and run the main loop. You can use functions like player.moveLeft(), player.moveUp(), player.interact(), etc.

Or get fancy with a while loop that takes input commands in real-time (though you can't backtrack if you die):

Enemies & Combat

As you descend floors, different enemies appear. It starts with regular rats, then white rats (faster variants), crabs (tankier with more HP), and eventually mimics—fake treasure chests that attack you.

Each enemy has its own stats. For example, a rat might have 4 HP per level, 2 ATK per level, and 80% hit chance. White rats trade some HP for higher movement speed. Crabs can move and attack in the same turn.

Oh, and there's a vault floor dice item that teleports you to a loot room—but sets your HP to 1. High risk, high reward.

infoPages.title: Map Editor
infoPages.content:
Build Your Own Dungeons

CodeDungeon has a built-in map editor. Launch it by passing 'Create' to player.startGame().

Click tiles to cycle through types:

  • 0 - Air (can spawn enemies/loot)
  • 1 - Wall
  • 2 - Player spawn
  • 3 - Exit
  • 4 - High loot chance
  • 5 - High enemy chance
  • 6 - Sign (with message)
  • 7 - NPC
  • 8 - Empty air
  • 9 - Boss fight

Export to console for easy editing, or directly to levelData.json. For advanced stuff—like locked doors, special loot, or custom enemy spawns—you edit the JSON directly. The system is ridiculously customizable.

Maps can be any size. Vertical, horizontal, tiny, huge—whatever you want.

infoPages.title: JSON & Customization
infoPages.content:
Customization Heaven

This was my second project after learning about JSON, and I went all in on making everything customizable. Enemy stats, loot tables, spawn chances, player progression—it's all in gameData.json.

Example: Rat Stats

"rat": {
  "enemy": {
    "statsPerLevel": {
      "HP": 4,
      "ATK": 2,
      "deathXP": 5
    },
    "lessATKpointsPercentage": 20,
    "hitChance": 80,
    "movementRules": {
      "attackRule": "insteadOf",
      "movement": 1,
      "attack": 1
    }
  }
}

Translation: 4 HP and 2 ATK per dungeon level. 80% hit chance. 20% damage variance. Can either move 1 tile or attack once per turn.

Loot System

Loot has rarity tiers (common, uncommon, rare, epic, legendary, impossible) with weighted spawn chances. You can find food (heals HP), weapons (better attack), keys, coins, and even special items like the vault floor dice.

Weapons have minimum strength requirements, attack values, and types (stab for single target, slice for AOE). You can merge items—like combining 5 moldy bread into 3 old bread—for better efficiency.

Updater

I even wrote an updater script to migrate old JSON formats to newer versions (works from v1.3.0+). Because of course I did.

Check out the full project on GitHub →

displayItems.title: My First Open Source PR
displayItems.description: Fixing a Quartz annoyance with my first PR.

infoPages.title: index
infoPages.content:
My First Open Source PR

I got annoyed. Like, really annoyed.

See, I use Quartz to host my Obsidian vault at 🌵OldMartijntje's Basement. In Obsidian, you can set image width using their markdown syntax: ![300](image.jpg). But when Quartz renders it, this didn't work. My images just stayed full-width, looking weird.

The workaround? Embedding raw HTML: <img src="" style="width:300px"/>. Annoying, right?

The Fix

I realized I could modify Quartz's links.ts file to support both the Obsidian syntax (![alt|300]()) and plain numbers (![300]()). So I did. Then I thought... why not submit this as a PR?

Turns out there was already an open issue about this exact thing. Perfect.

The Moment

I forked the repo, added the code, tested it, and submitted my first real pull request to a project I'm not part of. No big deal, just fixing something that was mildly infuriating me.

If you're using Quartz and want to try it out, check the PR. And hey, if you've got a small annoyance in an open source project, maybe it's worth fixing too.

displayItems.title: My new blog system
displayItems.description: How I decided to revamp my website because I got annoyed

infoPages.content: I am not going to spoil how, what and why. But there is a new blog system incoming, and it will have something to do with this. That is all the info that I will share rn.
